questions.text: In photo-emission from a metal, if light of λ is replaced by light of wavelength λ / 4 , the maximum kinetic energy of the photoelectrons.

options.text: Decreases by an amount equal to half of an incident photon of wavelength λ
options.explanation: When the wavelength of the incident light is decreased to λ/4 the energy of each photon increases. The energy of a photon is directly proportional to its frequency and inversely proportional to its wavelength. So, if the wavelength decreases to λ/4 the energy of each photon increases by a factor of 4. When a photoelectron is emitted, it gains energy from absorbing a photon. Since each photon now carries four times the energy compared to before, the photoelectron would gain four times the energy compared to when the wavelength was λ Thus, the correct choice would involve an increase in kinetic energy, not a decrease.

questions.text: If frequency of incident light is increased beyond threshold frequency, keeping light intensity constant, then:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: If the frequency of incident light is increased beyond the threshold frequency, while keeping the light intensity constant, the photoelectric effect will still occur, but the kinetic energy of the emitted electrons will increase.The photoelectric effect is the phenomenon where electrons are emitted from a material when it is exposed to light of sufficient energy. Each material has a specific threshold frequency, also known as the cutoff frequency, below which no electrons are emitted regardless of the intensity of the light. When the frequency of the incident light exceeds the threshold frequency, electrons are ejected from the material. The energy of the emitted electrons depends on the frequency of the incident light. According to the equation E = hf, where E is the energy of a photon, h is Planck's constant, and f is the frequency of the light, the energy of the emitted electrons is directly proportional to the frequency of the incident light.Therefore, if the frequency of the incident light is increased beyond the threshold frequency, the energy of the photons increases, leading to higher kinetic energy of the emitted electrons. The number of emitted electrons remains proportional to the intensity of the incident light, as long as the intensity is above the threshold intensity.In summary, increasing the frequency of incident light beyond the threshold frequency, while keeping the light intensity constant, results in higher kinetic energy for the emitted electrons in the photoelectric effect.

questions.text: Gamma-ray (𝝲) can produce ionization in which of the following way/s?I. It may lose all its energy in a single encounter with the electron of an atom (Photoelectric effect).II. It may lose only a part of its energy in an encounter (Compton effect).III. Very few very high energy 𝝲 ray photons may impinge directly on heavy nuclei, be stopped and annihilated giving rise to electron-positron pairs (The materialization of energy).
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Gamma ray photons can interact with matter in all of the ways described above. All three statements mentioned are correct.
Gamma ray photons can interact with matter in all of the ways described above. All three statements mentioned are correct.

Gamma rays (𝝲) can produce ionization in the following ways:I. Photoelectric Effect: In this process, a gamma-ray photon interacts with an electron bound to an atom. The gamma-ray transfers all its energy to the electron, causing the electron to be ejected from the atom. This results in the ionization of the atom. The ejected electron is called a photoelectron.II. Compton Effect (Compton Scattering): In this process, a gamma-ray photon collides with an electron in an atom. The gamma-ray transfers only a part of its energy to the electron, changing the direction and energy of both the gamma-ray and the electron. The gamma ray continues its path with reduced energy, and the electron is ejected from the atom, causing ionization.III. Materialization of Energy (Pair Production): For very high-energy gamma-ray photons, when they approach the vicinity of a heavy nucleus, they can spontaneously convert their energy into an electron and a positron (an antimatter counterpart of an electron). The gamma-ray photon ceases to exist, and the newly formed electron-positron pair carries the energy of the original gamma-ray. This process is known as pair production and results in the ionization of the material surrounding the heavy nucleus.So, all three processes (Photoelectric effect, Compton effect, and Pair Production) can lead to ionization by gamma rays. The likelihood of each process occurring depends on the energy of the gamma-ray and the properties of the interacting material.

questions.text: When photons are incident on a metal, electrons are emitted. The process is called:

X-ray production typically occurs when high-energy electrons strike a material and generate X-rays. Although X-rays can cause electrons to be emitted from a material, the process of generating X-rays is distinct from the photoelectric effect.

Gamma ray production involves the emission of high-energy photons from nuclear processes. While gamma rays can interact with materials to cause electron emissions, this process is not the same as the photoelectric effect.

Radioactive capture, or radiative capture, is a process where a nucleus captures a particle, often leading to nuclear reactions. It does not describe the emission of electrons from a metal due to incident photons.

According to the theory, an observer in motion relative to a clock that is at rest will measure the clock as ticking slower than if the observer were at rest relative to the clock.The observer in motion experiences time at a slower rate than the observer at rest. This effect becomes significant when the relative velocity of the observer and the clock approaches the speed of light, at which point time dilation becomes infinite. This means that an observer moving at the speed of light would perceive no passage of time at all.The proper time is the time interval measured by a clock that is at rest relative to the events being measured. It is the time interval measured by an observer who is in the same reference frame as the clock. The improper time is the time interval measured by a clock that is moving relative to the events being measured. It is the time interval measured by an observer who is in a different reference frame than the clock.In the given question, the observer moving with the velocity of light would measure the time interval differently than the observer at rest. Therefore, the observer moving with the velocity of light would measure the improper time.

questions.text: The uncertainty in momentum and position is due to its:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option refers to the inherent uncertainty principle proposed by Werner Heisenberg, known as the Heisenberg Uncertainty Principle. According to this principle, there is a fundamental limit to the precision with which certain pairs of physical properties, such as momentum and position, can be simultaneously known. So, this option correctly identifies the source of uncertainty in momentum and position.
Uncertainty in momentum and position is not inherently linked to two-dimensional motion. The Heisenberg Uncertainty Principle applies to particles in all dimensions of motion.

While extreme velocities can certainly affect our ability to measure momentum and position accurately, the Heisenberg Uncertainty Principle is a more fundamental explanation for the inherent uncertainty in these properties.
: The uncertainty in momentum and position is not solely due to the small size of particles. Even large objects, in principle, are subject to the Heisenberg Uncertainty Principle.

questions.explanation: In order to find the position and momentum of matter, we need to observe these metrics. In order to observe them, we need to have radiation reflect off of the matter after interacting with it. Hence to precisely know the measurements, we first must precisely know the nature of the radiation (wavelength and energy). However, in order to know the nature of radiation, we need it to interact with matter. Thus this juxtaposition stemming from the inherent nature of matter and radiation gives rise to the uncertainty principle.

questions.text: White light gives spectrum after undergoing:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: White light gives a spectrum after undergoing a process called dispersion. Dispersion is the phenomenon in which light is separated into its component colors (wavelengths) when it passes through a prism or a diffraction grating.White light is a combination of all visible colors in the electromagnetic spectrum. When white light passes through a prism, the different colors of light are refracted (bent) by different amounts due to their varying wavelengths. This causes the different colors to spread out and form a continuous spectrum, known as a rainbow or a spectrum of colors.The dispersion of white light into a spectrum occurs because the refractive index of a medium (such as glass in a prism) depends on the wavelength of light. As a result, shorter wavelengths (blue and violet) are bent more than longer wavelengths (red and orange), leading to the separation of colors.The resulting spectrum shows a continuous sequence of colors from violet, indigo, blue, green, yellow, orange, and red, with each color corresponding to a different wavelength of light.The phenomenon of dispersion is essential in many areas of science and technology, such as spectroscopy, which is the study of interactions between light and matter based on the analysis of light spectra. It also plays a crucial role in the formation of rainbows and other optical phenomena observed in nature.

questions.text: X-rays are used to investigate:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: X-rays are commonly used to investigate the structure of crystals. This is known as X-ray crystallography. When X-rays interact with a crystal lattice, they undergo diffraction patterns that can be analyzed to determine the arrangement of atoms within the crystal. This technique has been crucial in elucidating the structures of many important molecules, including proteins and complex organic compounds.

X-rays can also be used to investigate the structure of molecules. X-ray crystallography is particularly effective for determining the structures of large molecules or molecular complexes that can be crystallized. Additionally, techniques such as X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS) can provide information about the electronic structure and chemical bonding within molecules.

X-rays can be used to investigate ions, particularly in studies related to materials science, chemistry, and solid-state physics. Techniques such as X-ray diffraction (XRD) and X-ray spectroscopy can provide information about the arrangement of ions in materials, their electronic structure, and their chemical properties.

While X-rays are not commonly used to directly investigate electrons, they can indirectly provide information about electronic structure and behavior. For example, X-ray techniques such as X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and X-ray emission spectroscopy (XES) can provide valuable insights into the electronic structure and chemical bonding of materials, including the behavior of electrons.

questions.text: The existence of positron was discovered in the:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: An experiment on Cosmic rays performed by Carl D Anderson led to the accidental discovery of the positron. Anderson allowed cosmic rays to pass through a cloud chamber and a lead plate. A magnet surrounded this apparatus, caused the particles to bend in different directions based on their electric charge. The ion trail left by each positron appeared on the photographic plate with a curvature matching the mass-to-charge ratio of an electron, but in a direction that showed its charge was positive. It was the first evidence of anti-matter.

options.explanation: The photoelectric effect is the phenomenon where electrons are emitted from a material (usually a metal) when it is exposed to light or electromagnetic radiation of a sufficiently high frequency. The energy of the incident photons is absorbed by the material, causing electrons to be ejected from its surface. The photoelectric effect is the principle on which photocells are based. In a photocell, incident light causes the emission of electrons, generating an electric current.

The Compton effect, also known as Compton scattering, is the phenomenon where X-ray or gamma-ray photons interact with electrons in matter. During this interaction, the photon transfers some of its energy and momentum to the electron, leading to a change in the photon's wavelength and direction. The Compton effect is not directly related to the operation of a photocell.

Photoluminescence is a process in which a material absorbs photons (light) and then re-emits the absorbed energy as light of a longer wavelength. This emission of light is called luminescence. Photoluminescence is seen in phenomena like fluorescence and phosphorescence. It is not directly related to the function of a photocell.

questions.text: Which light photon has the least momentum?

options.explanation: Red light has the longest wavelength of all visible light, about 700 nanometers. The momentum of a photon is inversely proportional to its wavelength, so the photon with the longest wavelength will have the least momentum. Therefore, the momentum of a red photon is the least of all visible light photons.
Green light has a wavelength of about 550 nanometers. This is shorter than the wavelength of red light, so the momentum of a green photon is greater than the momentum of a red photon.

Yellow light has a wavelength of about 580 nanometers. This is between the wavelengths of red and green light, so the momentum of a yellow photon is between the momentum of a red photon and the momentum of a green photon.
Blue light has a wavelength of about 400 nanometers. This is the shortest wavelength of all visible light, so the momentum of a blue photon is the greatest of all visible light photons.

questions.text: A photosensitive material would emit electrons if it is excited by a photon beyond a threshold. To cross the threshold, what should be increased?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The wavelength of light determines the energy of the photons. Shorter wavelengths correspond to higher energy photons, according to the equation E = hc/λ, where E is energy, h is Planck's constant, c is the speed of light, and λ is the wavelength. To overcome the threshold energy and cause electron emission, the energy of the photon must be greater than the work function of the material. Increasing the wavelength of light (which means decreasing its energy) would not help to overcome the threshold energy; it would, in fact, move the photon's energy further away from the required threshold.

Frequency and wavelength are inversely related in electromagnetic waves. Increasing the frequency of light corresponds to decreasing the wavelength, which, as mentioned above, increases the energy of the photons. So, increasing the frequency of light would indeed help to overcome the threshold energy and cause electron emission. This is because higher frequency light carries more energy per photon, making it more likely to surpass the material's work function.

This option refers to applying an external voltage to the photosensitive material. Increasing the voltage across the material can provide additional energy to the electrons within it. However, this doesn't directly affect the photoelectric effect. While an external voltage can influence the kinetic energy of emitted electrons (by accelerating them), it doesn't change the energy of the incident photons. So, while increasing voltage can affect the behavior of emitted electrons, it's not directly related to overcoming the threshold energy of the photoelectric effect.

The intensity of light refers to the amount of light energy per unit area per unit time. Increasing the intensity of light means increasing the number of photons incident on the material per unit time. However, intensity alone doesn't affect the energy of individual photons. Therefore, while increasing the intensity might result in more electrons being emitted (since more photons are striking the material), it doesn't help in overcoming the threshold energy for individual photons.

questions.explanation: Einstein's Photoelectric Effect equation is: hf = hfo + KEmax Where hf is the energy of the photon, hfo is the work function of the material, and KEmax is the surplus energy that enables the electron to leave the surface of the material. The work function (hfo) is the minimum or threshold energy required to cause the photoelectric effect. If the energy of a photon is less than hfo, not enough energy would be transmitted to the electron to come up to the surface, and there would be no photoelectric effect. Thus by increasing the frequency and decreasing the wavelength, the threshold for the photoelectric effect can be crossed. The intensity of light does not affect the threshold. It only affects the number of electrons emitted and hence, the photocurrent. The voltage of the source affects current; increasing the positive voltage (when the cathode is negative) increases the current while increasing the negative voltage (when the cathode is positive) decreases the current. At a particular value of negative voltage, called the stopping potential, the current becomes zero.

questions.text: Michelson determined the velocity of light by:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Michelson did not determine the velocity of light by studying the rotation of the moon and sun. While the motion of celestial bodies can provide valuable astronomical data, it's not directly related to determining the velocity of light.

An interferometer is a device that splits a beam of light into two beams and then recombines them. The two beams travel different paths, and the interference pattern produced when they are recombined can be used to measure the difference in the path lengths. This method can be used to measure the speed of light with high accuracy. However, was not used by Michelson.

While Michelson used mirrors in his experiments, he didn't specifically use a rotating plane mirror to determine the velocity of light. Instead, he used a system of mirrors in conjunction with the interferometer setup.

options.text: Using an octagonal rotating mirror D
options.explanation: Michelson determined the velocity of light using an experiment known as the Michelson-Morley experiment, which involved an interferometer.However, the specific method Michelson used involved a rotating octagonal mirror, not just any rotating plane mirror.In the experiment, light was split into two beams, one that traveled along the direction of Earth's motion through the hypothetical "ether" and one that traveled perpendicular to it. These beams were then recombined using mirrors, and interference patterns were observed. By comparing the interference patterns at different times of the year when the Earth's velocity relative to the supposed ether changed, Michelson sought to detect any variation in the speed of light. questions.text: The number of photoelectrons emitted from metal depends upon:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The intensity of incident light refers to the brightness or the amount of light energy falling on the metal surface per unit area per unit time. According to the photoelectric effect, the number of photoelectrons emitted from a metal surface depends directly on the intensity of the incident light. Higher-intensity light means more photons, thus more photoelectrons are emitted. However, the energy of individual photons remains the same.

The energy of the incident light (i.e., the energy carried by each photon) plays a significant role in the photoelectric effect. For photoelectrons to be emitted from the metal, the energy of the incident photons must be equal to or greater than the work function of the metal (the minimum energy required to remove an electron from the metal's surface). Only photons with sufficient energy can cause photoemission.

The wavelength of the incident light is directly related to its energy. Shorter wavelengths (higher energy photons) have a greater chance of causing photoemission compared to longer wavelengths with lower energies. The energy of a photon is directly proportional to the frequency of light and inversely proportional to its wavelength.

The frequency of the incident light determines the energy of the individual photons. Photons with higher frequencies (corresponding to shorter wavelengths) have more energy and are more likely to cause the emission of photoelectrons.

questions.text: The X-ray photon is uncertain when it is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: When an X-ray photon is emitted, its exact position and momentum cannot be precisely determined simultaneously due to the principles of quantum mechanics, specifically the Heisenberg Uncertainty Principle. The uncertainty principle states that there is a fundamental limit to the precision with which certain pairs of physical properties, such as position and momentum, can be simultaneously known. Therefore, the position and momentum of an X-ray photon are uncertain when it is emitted.

Similarly, when an X-ray photon is absorbed by a material or an object, its position and momentum become uncertain. The act of absorption involves interactions at the quantum level, where the exact location and momentum of the photon cannot be precisely determined simultaneously.

Even while the X-ray photon is traveling through space or a medium, its position and momentum are subject to uncertainty. The photon's trajectory and speed cannot be precisely known at any given moment due to the inherent probabilistic nature of quantum mechanics.

questions.text: From the following properties of a wave, the one that is independent of the other three is its?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The amplitude of a wave refers to the maximum displacement or the maximum value of the oscillating quantity from the mean position. It is independent of the other properties of the wave because it represents the intensity or strength of the wave. Regardless of the frequency, wavelength, or time period, the amplitude can vary independently, affecting the wave's intensity without altering its frequency or wavelength.

The frequency of a wave is the number of oscillations or cycles it completes in a unit of time. It is inversely proportional to the wavelength, as higher frequencies correspond to shorter wavelengths and vice versa. However, it is not independent of the other properties since it is directly related to the time period (frequency = 1 / time period) and inversely related to wavelength (frequency = speed of wave/wavelength). Thus, frequency is dependent on both wavelength and time period.

The wavelength of a wave is the distance between two consecutive points in the wave that are in phase. It is inversely proportional to frequency, meaning that as frequency increases, wavelength decreases, and vice versa. Wavelength is also related to the wave's speed by the equation speed = frequency × wavelength. Therefore, wavelength is not independent of the other properties, as it is related to frequency and speed.

The time period of a wave is the time taken for one complete oscillation or cycle. It is the reciprocal of frequency (time period = 1 / frequency). As mentioned earlier, frequency is inversely proportional to the time period, so they are closely related. Time period is also related to wavelength and wave speed through the equation speed = wavelength/time period. Therefore, the time period is not independent of the other properties of the wave.

questions.explanation: The amplitude of a wave represents its intensity or strength, and it does not depend on frequency, wavelength, or time period. For example, you can have a high-amplitude wave with a low frequency, long wavelength, and long time period, or you can have a low-amplitude wave with a high frequency, short wavelength, and short time period. The intensity of the wave can be adjusted independently of these other properties.

questions.text: Which light photon has the least momentum?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Red light has the longest wavelength of all visible light, about 700 nanometers. The momentum of a photon is inversely proportional to its wavelength, so the photon with the longest wavelength will have the least momentum. Therefore, the momentum of a red photon is the least of all visible light photons.
Green light has a wavelength of about 550 nanometers. This is shorter than the wavelength of red light, so the momentum of a green photon is greater than the momentum of a red photon.

Yellow light has a wavelength of about 580 nanometers. This is between the wavelengths of red and green light, so the momentum of a yellow photon is between the momentum of a red photon and the momentum of a green photon.
Blue light has a wavelength of about 400 nanometers. This is the shortest wavelength of all visible light, so the momentum of a blue photon is the greatest of all visible light photons.

questions.explanation: The light photon with the least momentum is red. This is because red light has the longest wavelength of all visible light. The momentum of a photon is inversely proportional to its wavelength, so the photon with the longest wavelength will have the least momentum.The momentum of a photon can be calculated using the following equation:p = h/λwhere p is the momentum, h is Planck's constant, and λ is the wavelength.The wavelength of red light is about 700 nanometers, while the wavelength of blue light is about 400 nanometers. This means that the momentum of a red photon is about half the momentum of a blue photon.Therefore, the light photon with the least momentum is red.

questions.text: Wave-particle duality does not explain:

options.explanation: Wave-particle duality does explain momentum. In the wave-like aspect, particles can exhibit wave properties such as wavelength and frequency, which are related to momentum. In the particle-like aspect, particles have mass and velocity, both of which are components of momentum.

Wave-particle duality is directly related to the concept of frequency. In the wave-like aspect, particles can exhibit wave properties such as frequency, which is the number of wave cycles or oscillations per unit of time. This frequency is associated with the wave nature of particles.

Wave-particle duality is a fundamental concept in quantum mechanics that states that particles, such as electrons or photons, can exhibit both wave-like and particle-like properties depending on how they are observed or measured. While wave-particle duality helps explain certain phenomena, it does not directly explain the concept of mass.Mass is a fundamental property of matter and is associated with the amount of matter present in an object. It is not a property that arises solely from the wave-like or particle-like nature of a particle. Mass is a separate characteristic that is independent of the wave-particle duality concept.

This option is incorrect because wave-particle duality does provide an explanation for momentum and frequency, as explained above. However, it does not directly explain the concept of mass, which is a separate property.

questions.explanation: In quantum physics, wave-particle duality states that any particle or quantum phenomenon can be represented as either a particle or a wave. It expresses the difficulty of classical terminology like "particle" and "wave" to completely characterize quantum-scale things' behavior.This behavior has been observed not just in elementary particles but also in composite particles such as atoms and molecules. Wave characteristics are frequently not detectable for macroscopic particles due to their relatively short wavelengths.As a result, mass isn't included because quantum mechanics doesn't contain it.

questions.explanation: In Compton's scattering process, the wavelength of scattered X-ray increases. Compton scattering is a type of scattering of electromagnetic radiation by a charged particle, usually an electron. In this process, a photon (x-ray) collides with an electron, and the photon transfers some of its energy and momentum to the electron. As a result, the photon's wavelength increases, and its frequency decreases. The change in wavelength is given by the Compton formula: Δλ = h/mc(1 - cosθ), where Δλ is the change in wavelength, h is Planck's constant, m is the mass of the electron, c is the speed of light, and θ is the angle between the incident photon and the scattered photon.

options.explanation: In an inertial frame, objects will continue to move at a constant velocity if no external forces act upon them. Therefore, in an inertial frame, objects can indeed have constant velocity.

While objects in an inertial frame can have zero velocity, it's not a defining characteristic of an inertial frame. An inertial frame can accommodate objects with various velocities, including zero velocity, as long as they maintain their state of motion (or lack thereof) unless acted upon by external forces.

In an inertial frame, objects experiencing no external forces will have zero acceleration. This is consistent with Newton's first law, which implies that in the absence of external forces, an object's velocity (and hence acceleration) will remain constant. However, it's important to note that objects can have non-zero velocities in an inertial frame, but if they do, they will continue with that velocity unless acted upon by external forces.

options.explanation: a) Continuous: This option is correct. A blackbody emits radiation across a continuous range of wavelengths or frequencies. This results in a continuous spectrum without any gaps or breaks, often referred to as a blackbody radiation curve or a Planck curve.

questions.text: Which radiation passes through black body

options.explanation: Alpha radiation consists of helium nuclei and is unable to penetrate materials easily. It is primarily stopped by a sheet of paper or the outer layer of human skin.

Gamma radiation is highly penetrating and can pass through many materials, but it does not pass through a black body as a whole. The term 'black body' refers to an idealized physical body that absorbs all incident electromagnetic radiation.
Beta radiation consists of electrons or positrons. While beta particles can penetrate materials better than alpha particles, they are still not capable of passing through a black body.

questions.explanation: A black body is an idealized physical object that absorbs all radiation that falls on it. This means that it does not reflect or transmit any radiation, effectively making it impossible for any radiation to pass through it. In contrast, alpha particles are large and positively charged, thus unable to penetrate even small barriers; beta particles, being smaller and negatively charged, can penetrate more than alpha particles but still cannot pass through a black body; gamma radiation, while highly penetrating, will not pass through a black body due to its defining characteristic of complete absorption. Therefore, the correct answer is that no radiation can pass through a black body.

questions.text: Which of the following physical phenomena connot be described only by the wave theory of the electromagnetic radiation?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Diffraction involves the bending and spreading of waves when they encounter an obstacle or opening. This phenomenon is well-explained by the wave theory of electromagnetic radiation, as it results in patterns of constructive and destructive interference that align with the wave nature of light.

Interference is a result of the superposition of waves from coherent sources. This phenomenon, demonstrated in Young's Double-Slit Experiment, is a classic example of the wave nature of light, where overlapping waves produce regions of reinforcement and cancellation.

The photoelectric effect occurs when light strikes a metal surface, causing the emission of electrons. This phenomenon cannot be described by wave theory alone, as it requires the concept of photons—discrete packets of energy. Einstein explained this effect using the particle nature of light, highlighting that energy is transferred in quantized amounts.

Polarization is the process by which the oscillations of light waves are restricted to a single plane. This phenomenon indicates the transverse nature of light waves and is consistent with the wave theory of electromagnetic radiation, as it does not require a particle-like description.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is the Photoelectric Effect. This phenomenon cannot be adequately described by the wave theory of light alone because it demonstrates that light can behave as a stream of particles (photons), with each photon having a discrete energy that can be transferred to electrons. This is in contrast to wave-based phenomena like diffraction, interference, and polarization, which can be fully explained by the wave theory of electromagnetic radiation.

The stopping potential is directly related to the kinetic energy of the ejected protons. When the stopping potential is applied, it counteracts the kinetic energy of the electrons, eventually bringing them to a stop. questions.text: In pair annihilation, two gamma-ray photons created, travel in opposite directions not in the same direction, because:

options.text: This proves law of conservation of energy
options.explanation: The law of energy conservation states that energy is neither created nor destroyed. It may transform from one type to another.

options.text: This proves law of conservation of momentum
options.explanation: The law of conservation of momentum states that in an isolated system, the total momentum of two or more bodies acting upon each other remains constant unless an external force is applied. Therefore, momentum can neither be created nor destroyed. In pair annihilation, for conservation of momentum, the momentum after annihilation must be zero, and the only way that this can happen is for the two photons to be emitted in opposite directions such that their same amount of individual momentum cancels each other out.

options.text: This proves law of conservation of charge
options.explanation: Conservation of charge is the principle that the total electric charge in an isolated system never changes. In pair production, the positron, +e, as a particle, has the same properties which an electron has, except its charge sign; the two particles, electron and positron, have the opposite charge, and thus its magnetic momentum sign is also opposite. Having an opposite charge sign means that the sum of the net charge of pairs is zero, which is actually equal to the photon before the collision. Therefore, the conservation of electric charge will be conserved.

options.text: This proves law of conservation of mass-energy
options.explanation: The law of conservation of matter and the law of conservation of energy state that matter or energy cannot be created or destroyed but can be converted to different forms or get rearranged.

questions.text: If the wavelength of incident radiation in a photoelectric experiment is decreased then
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The photoelectric current will decrease – Incorrect: The photoelectric current depends on the intensity of light, not its wavelength. Decreasing the wavelength increases the photon energy but does not necessarily reduce current.

The photoelectric current will increase – Incorrect: The number of emitted electrons (current) depends on intensity, not wavelength. Decreasing the wavelength increases energy but does not necessarily increase the number of emitted electrons

The stopping potential will decrease – Incorrect: Stopping potential depends on the maximum kinetic energy of the emitted electrons. As wavelength decreases, photon energy increases, so stopping potential should increase.

The stopping potential will increase – Correct: According to Einstein’s photoelectric equation KEmax=hf−ϕ, decreasing the wavelength increases frequency (f), which increases the kinetic energy of emitted electrons. This results in a higher stopping potential needed to stop them.

questions.explanation: The stopping potential will increase – Correct: According to Einstein’s photoelectric equation KEmax=hf−ϕ, decreasing the wavelength increases frequency (f), which increases the kinetic energy of emitted electrons. This results in a higher stopping potential needed to stop them.

questions.text: In a photoelectric effect experiment the stopping potential is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.text: The electric potential that causes the electronic current to vanish
options.explanation: The electric potential that causes the electronic current to vanish – Correct: Stopping potential is the voltage needed to stop even the most energetic electrons from reaching the anode, making the photoelectric current zero
The photon energy – Incorrect: The photon energy is given by hf, but stopping potential is related to the kinetic energy of the emitted electrons.

options.text: The kinetic energy of the most energetic electron ejected
options.explanation: The kinetic energy of the most energetic electron ejected – Incorrect: Stopping potential is related to kinetic energy, but it is the voltage that prevents electron motion, not the energy itself.

options.text: The energy required to remove an electron from the sample
options.explanation: The energy required to remove an electron from the sample – Incorrect: The energy required to remove an electron is the work function (ϕ), while stopping potential depends on the additional kinetic energy electrons gain after removal

questions.explanation: The electric potential that causes the electronic current to vanish – Correct: Stopping potential is the voltage needed to stop even the most energetic electrons from reaching the anode, making the photoelectric current zero

questions.text: The speed of light in the vacuum is the same for all observers regardless of their relative motion or the motion of the source of the light", is the important postulate of:

options.explanation: Special conductivity – Incorrect: There is no such physical concept related to relativity.
Special resistivity – Incorrect: This term does not relate to Einstein’s postulates.
Special theory of relativity – Correct: Einstein’s special theory of relativity states that the speed of light is constant for all observers, regardless of their motion or the motion of the source.
